exercises.title: Trace two pointers on sorted array
exercises.instructions:
Trace the two-pointer execution for:
`twoSum([2, 7, 11, 15], target=9)`

Show each step: lo index, hi index, sum, decision.
exercises.expected_solution:
Array: [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9
lo=0, hi=3

Step 1: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=15, sum=17 > 9 → hi-- (sum too big)
Step 2: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=11, sum=13 > 9 → hi-- (sum too big)
Step 3: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=7,  sum=9  = 9 → FOUND! [indices 0,1]

Answer: indices [0, 1] (values 2 and 7)
Total steps: 3 (not n²=16)

Key insight:
  We eliminated index 3 (value 15) after step 1:
  ANY pair with 15 gives sum ≥ 2+15=17 > 9
  So 15 can NEVER be part of a valid pair → safe to discard
  This is why two pointers is correct, not just fast.

test_cases.expected:
Array: [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9
lo=0, hi=3

Step 1: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=15, sum=17 > 9 → hi-- (sum too big)
Step 2: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=11, sum=13 > 9 → hi-- (sum too big)
Step 3: arr[lo]=2, arr[hi]=7,  sum=9  = 9 → FOUND! [indices 0,1]

Answer: indices [0, 1] (values 2 and 7)
Total steps: 3 (not n²=16)

Key insight:
  We eliminated index 3 (value 15) after step 1:
  ANY pair with 15 gives sum ≥ 2+15=17 > 9
  So 15 can NEVER be part of a valid pair → safe to discard
  This is why two pointers is correct, not just fast.

hints: Start: lo=0, hi=n-1
sum > target: hi-- (make sum smaller)
sum < target: lo++ (make sum bigger)

exercises.title: Which problems suit two pointers?
exercises.instructions:
For each problem, state: Two Pointers (TP), Sliding Window (SW), or Neither (N):
A: Find if sorted array has pair summing to K
B: Find maximum sum of exactly K consecutive elements
C: Check if string is palindrome
D: Find all triplets summing to zero in unsorted array
E: Find first occurrence of character 'x'
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Find pair summing to K in SORTED array → TWO POINTERS
   Classic inward TP on sorted: lo=0, hi=n-1

B: Max sum of exactly K consecutive elements → SLIDING WINDOW
   Fixed-size window: not a pair problem, continuous subarray

C: Check if string is palindrome → TWO POINTERS
   lo from left, hi from right: check chars match, move inward
   O(n) time, O(1) space — classic TP on string

D: All triplets summing to 0 in unsorted array → SORT + TWO POINTERS
   Sort array O(n log n), fix one element, TP on remainder O(n)
   Total: O(n²) — still better than O(n³) brute force

E: Find first 'x' → NEITHER
   Simple linear scan: O(n), no TP needed
   TP adds no value here

test_cases.expected:
A: Find pair summing to K in SORTED array → TWO POINTERS
   Classic inward TP on sorted: lo=0, hi=n-1

B: Max sum of exactly K consecutive elements → SLIDING WINDOW
   Fixed-size window: not a pair problem, continuous subarray

C: Check if string is palindrome → TWO POINTERS
   lo from left, hi from right: check chars match, move inward
   O(n) time, O(1) space — classic TP on string

D: All triplets summing to 0 in unsorted array → SORT + TWO POINTERS
   Sort array O(n log n), fix one element, TP on remainder O(n)
   Total: O(n²) — still better than O(n³) brute force

E: Find first 'x' → NEITHER
   Simple linear scan: O(n), no TP needed
   TP adds no value here

hints: TP: sorted array, looking for pair/triplet
SW: contiguous subarray with some property
If array is unsorted: sort first, then apply TP

exercises.title: Two Sum II — sorted array (LeetCode #167)
exercises.instructions:
Given a 1-indexed sorted array, find two numbers summing to target. Return their 1-based indices.

Example: numbers=[2,7,11,15], target=9 → [1,2]

Must use O(1) extra space. Guaranteed exactly one solution.
hints: lo=0, hi=n-1 (0-indexed). Add 1 for 1-indexed answer
sum < target: lo++ (move to larger value)
sum > target: hi-- (move to smaller value)

exercises.title: Valid Palindrome (LeetCode #125)
exercises.instructions:
A string is a palindrome if, after converting to lowercase and removing non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward.

Example: 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama' → true
Example: 'race a car' → false

Use two pointers. O(n) time, O(1) space.
hints: Skip non-alphanumeric: inner while loop on each side
Character.isLetterOrDigit(): skips spaces, commas, colons
Character.toLowerCase(): case-insensitive comparison

exercises.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array (LeetCode #26)
exercises.instructions:
Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in-place. Return the count of unique elements. The first k elements must contain unique values in order.

Example: [1,1,2] → k=2, array starts [1,2,...]
Example: [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4] → k=5, array starts [0,1,2,3,4,...]

O(n) time, O(1) space. Use slow/fast same-direction pointers.
hints: slow: write position for next unique element
fast: reads every element
nums[fast] != nums[fast-1]: new unique element found → write it

exercises.title: Container With Most Water (LeetCode #11)
exercises.instructions:
Given heights array, find two lines forming a container with max water.
volume = min(height[lo], height[hi]) × (hi - lo)

Example: height=[1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7] → 49

O(n) two pointers. Move the SHORTER side inward.
hints: Water = min(height[lo], height[hi]) × (hi - lo)
Move shorter pointer: moving taller pointer can only reduce min height AND width
Greedy proof: we never discard the optimal pair

exercises.title: Three Sum (LeetCode #15)
exercises.instructions:
Find all unique triplets in array summing to 0.

Example: nums=[-1,0,1,2,-1,-4] → [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]]

Approach: sort, fix one element, two-pointer on rest.
O(n²) time — but much better than O(n³) brute force.
Handle duplicates: skip repeated values for fixed element and both pointers.
hints: Sort first: enables two pointers AND easy duplicate skipping
Skip: nums[i]==nums[i-1] for fixed element (skip duplicate fixed elements)
After finding triplet: skip duplicate lo and hi values before moving pointers

exercises.title: Move Zeroes (LeetCode #283)
exercises.instructions:
Move all zeros to the end of array while maintaining relative order of non-zero elements. Must be in-place.

Example: [0,1,0,3,12] → [1,3,12,0,0]

O(n) time, O(1) space. Same-direction two pointers.
hints: slow: next write position for non-zero values
fast: reads every element
After moving non-zeros: fill remainder with zeros (overwrite what was there)

exercises.title: Squares of Sorted Array (LeetCode #977)
exercises.instructions:
Given sorted array of integers (may contain negatives), return array of squares in sorted order.

Example: [-4,-1,0,3,10] → [0,1,9,16,100]

Naive: square all → sort → O(n log n). Two pointers from ends: O(n).
Hint: Largest squares come from either end (most negative or most positive).
hints: Largest squares: from either end (most negative or most positive)
Fill result from back: pos=n-1, decrement as we place elements
Compare lSq vs rSq: place larger one at current back position

exercises.title: Analyse three approaches to Two Sum on unsorted array
exercises.instructions:
Problem: find if any two elements sum to target in an UNSORTED array.

Approach A: Brute force nested loops
Approach B: Sort then two pointers
Approach C: HashSet

For each: state time complexity, space complexity, modifies input?, returns indices or just boolean?
exercises.expected_solution:
Approach A: Brute force
  Time: O(n²)
  Space: O(1)
  Modifies input: No
  Returns: can return exact indices (i, j)
  When to use: n < 1000, or when you need exact indices AND cannot sort

Approach B: Sort + Two Pointers
  Time: O(n log n) — dominated by sort
  Space: O(1) extra (or O(n) if sort creates copy)
  Modifies input: YES (sorts in place) — may be problem!
  Returns: can find values but NOT original indices (sorting changes positions)
  When to use: n > 10000, space constrained, original indices not needed
  Problem: destroys original index information

Approach C: HashSet
  Time: O(n)
  Space: O(n) — HashSet stores seen elements
  Modifies input: No
  Returns: can return original indices by using HashMap<value, index>
  When to use: MOST cases — fastest, doesn't modify input, preserves indices

RECOMMENDATION:
  Default choice: Approach C (HashSet/HashMap)
  Space constrained: Approach B (sort + TP)
  Need original indices AND cannot use extra space: Approach A (small n only)

Common interview follow-up:
  'Return the INDICES of the two numbers' → HashMap<value, index> → O(n) time/space
  'Array is already sorted' → Two pointers O(n) time O(1) space

test_cases.expected:
Approach A: Brute force
  Time: O(n²)
  Space: O(1)
  Modifies input: No
  Returns: can return exact indices (i, j)
  When to use: n < 1000, or when you need exact indices AND cannot sort

Approach B: Sort + Two Pointers
  Time: O(n log n) — dominated by sort
  Space: O(1) extra (or O(n) if sort creates copy)
  Modifies input: YES (sorts in place) — may be problem!
  Returns: can find values but NOT original indices (sorting changes positions)
  When to use: n > 10000, space constrained, original indices not needed
  Problem: destroys original index information

Approach C: HashSet
  Time: O(n)
  Space: O(n) — HashSet stores seen elements
  Modifies input: No
  Returns: can return original indices by using HashMap<value, index>
  When to use: MOST cases — fastest, doesn't modify input, preserves indices

RECOMMENDATION:
  Default choice: Approach C (HashSet/HashMap)
  Space constrained: Approach B (sort + TP)
  Need original indices AND cannot use extra space: Approach A (small n only)

Common interview follow-up:
  'Return the INDICES of the two numbers' → HashMap<value, index> → O(n) time/space
  'Array is already sorted' → Two pointers O(n) time O(1) space

hints: Sort + TP destroys original indices
HashMap: stores value→index mapping for O(1) lookup of complement
Best default: HashSet/HashMap O(n) time and space

exercises.title: Two Sum return indices (LeetCode #1)
exercises.instructions:
Given unsorted array and target, return INDICES of two numbers summing to target. Exactly one solution guaranteed.

Example: nums=[2,7,11,15], target=9 → [0,1]

O(n) time using HashMap. NOT O(n²) brute force.
hints: complement = target - nums[i]: what value do we need?
seen.containsKey(complement): did we already see this value?
Put AFTER check: prevents using same element twice (unless duplicate values)

exercises.title: Trapping Rain Water (LeetCode #42) — hard classic
exercises.instructions:
Given elevation map heights[], compute total water trapped after rain.

Example: height=[0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1] → 6

Two pointer approach: O(n) time, O(1) space.
Key: water at position i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]
hints: Process from the side with shorter current height
maxLeft/maxRight: tallest wall seen so far from each direction
water at lo = maxLeft - height[lo] (when maxLeft > height[lo])

exercises.title: Four Sum (LeetCode #18)
exercises.instructions:
Find all unique quadruplets summing to target.

Example: nums=[1,0,-1,0,-2,2], target=0 → [[-2,-1,1,2],[-2,0,0,2],[-1,0,0,1]]

Extend three sum: fix two elements (O(n²) outer), two-pointer on rest (O(n)).
Total: O(n³). Handle all duplicates.
hints: Use (long) sum: int overflow for large values × 4
Skip duplicates at all 4 levels: i, j, lo, hi
Pattern: K-sum → K-2 outer loops + two pointers → O(n^(K-1))

exercises.title: Minimum Window Substring — two pointer setup (LeetCode #76)
exercises.instructions:
Find the minimum length substring of s containing all characters of t.

Example: s='ADOBECODEBANC', t='ABC' → 'BANC'

This is a sliding window problem but uses two pointer mechanics.
Build frequency maps, expand right, contract left when all chars covered.
hints: need[]: how many of each char we require
required: count of unsatisfied chars (decrements when covered)
Contract: while required==0, try to shrink window from left

exercises.title: Complete M30 T1 Two Pointers Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Two Pointer toolkit — solve all 5 problems and verify:
1. twoSumSorted(sorted, target) → [i,j] or null
2. threeSum(nums) → all unique triplets summing to 0
3. isPalindrome(s) → true/false
4. maxWater(heights) → max container volume
5. moveZeroes(arr) → in-place zeros to end

Run all 5 with test cases. Print complexities. All must pass.
hints: Each of the 5 is a distinct two-pointer variant
threeSum: sort first, fix i, TP on i+1..n-1
All should be O(n) or O(n²) — no O(n³)
